Best Car Accessories for Florida — Sun, Heat, and Hurricanes

  1. Home
  2. /Blog
  3. /Best Car Accessories for Florida — Sun, Heat, and Hurricanes

Published April 12, 2026

Best Car Accessories for Florida — Sun, Heat, and Hurricanes

Your car takes a beating in Florida. The sun bakes it 300+ days a year. The steering wheel hits 160°F in a parking lot. The UV destroys your dashboard, fades your paint, and cracks your leather. And then from June to November, you might need to drive through a hurricane evacuation with everything you own in the back.

On top of the climate, Florida drivers are — let's be honest — unpredictable. We have tourists who don't know where they're going, snowbirds going 45 in the left lane, and aggressive locals weaving through traffic on I-275. A dash cam isn't paranoia here. It's evidence.

Here's the gear that every Florida car needs.

Sun and Heat Protection

1. EcoNour Windshield Sun Shade (Best Sun Shade — Mandatory)

The EcoNour Windshield Sun Shade is a thick, reflective windshield cover that blocks UV and heat from entering your car while parked. Available in custom sizes for most vehicles.

Why this is mandatory in Florida: Your car's interior reaches 140-170°F in a Florida parking lot. Without a sun shade, your steering wheel becomes untouchable, your leather/vinyl seats burn your legs, and your dashboard cracks within a year. A sun shade drops interior temperature by 30-40°F. Every car in Florida should have one. This is the single most important car accessory on this list.

Pro tip: Get one that actually fits your windshield — generic one-size-fits-all shades leave gaps that let heat in. Measure your windshield or buy a custom-fit version.

2. Gorla Gear Steering Wheel Cover (Best for Hot Steering Wheels)

The Gorla Gear Steering Wheel Cover adds a breathable layer between your hands and a scorching steering wheel. The mesh material doesn't absorb heat the way leather or vinyl does.

Why it matters for Florida: Even with a sun shade, if any sun reaches your steering wheel, it'll be uncomfortably hot. A breathable cover means you can grip the wheel immediately instead of waiting 5 minutes for the AC to cool it down. Choose a mesh or microfiber material — avoid leather covers that get just as hot as the wheel itself.

3. FH Group Neoprene Seat Covers (Best Seat Covers for Florida)

FH Group Neoprene Seat Covers protect your factory seats from UV damage, sweat, sunscreen stains, and beach sand while staying cooler than leather or vinyl surfaces.

Why they matter for Florida: Leather seats in a Florida car burn your skin. Vinyl cracks from UV exposure. Neoprene seat covers stay cooler to the touch, protect your factory upholstery from the constant sunscreen/sweat/sand exposure of Florida life, and wash easily. They're especially valuable if you go to the beach regularly and don't want sand permanently embedded in your seats.

4. 3M Crystalline Window Tint (Best Window Tint — Get This Done)

While not an Amazon product, ceramic window tint film is the most impactful heat reduction you can add to your Florida car. Professional installation of 3M Crystalline or equivalent ceramic tint on all windows blocks up to 97% of infrared heat and 99.9% of UV rays while remaining legal.

Why it's essential for Florida: Florida law allows 28% visible light transmission on front side windows and 15% on rear. Get the darkest legal tint on every window. It reduces interior temperature dramatically, protects your skin from UV during the daily commute, and cuts AC workload (improving fuel economy). Budget $300-$500 for professional ceramic tint — it's worth every penny.

Safety and Dash Cams

5. Viofo A129 Pro Duo Dash Cam (Best Dash Cam for Florida)

The Viofo A129 Pro Duo records front and rear simultaneously in 4K/1080P with GPS tracking. It includes a parking mode that records if someone hits your parked car, and a heat-resistant design rated to 158°F.

Why you need a dash cam in Florida: Florida is a no-fault insurance state, but having video evidence is still crucial for accidents, road rage incidents, and insurance claims. Tampa Bay traffic on I-275, I-75, and the Howard Frankland Bridge produces daily incidents. The heat-resistant rating matters — regular dash cams can fail or melt in Florida car interiors. The parking mode catches hit-and-runs in parking lots, which are shockingly common.

6. Lytmi Car Emergency Kit (Best Hurricane/Emergency Kit)

A comprehensive car emergency kit should live in your trunk year-round, but especially during hurricane season (June-November). Your kit should include:

  • Jumper cables or a NOCO Boost Plus GB40 Jump Starter (battery powered, no second car needed)
  • First aid kit
  • Flashlight and extra batteries
  • Reflective triangles
  • Basic tools
  • Phone charger cable
  • Water bottles (replace monthly in Florida heat — plastic leaches in hot trunks)
  • Non-perishable snacks

Why it matters for Florida: During hurricane evacuations, you might sit in traffic for 6-10 hours on I-75 or I-4. Gas stations run out of fuel. Your car could overheat. A jump starter, water, and basic supplies aren't just smart — they could be critical. The NOCO jump starter is especially valuable because it doesn't need another car and holds a charge for months.

7. JACO ElitePro Tire Pressure Gauge (Best Tire Pressure Monitor)

The JACO ElitePro Digital Tire Pressure Gauge gives accurate readings so you can check your tire pressure regularly.

Why it matters for Florida: Heat affects tire pressure. For every 10°F increase in temperature, tire pressure increases by about 1 PSI. Florida pavement temperatures create significant pressure changes between early morning and afternoon driving. Over-inflated tires reduce grip (dangerous in sudden afternoon thunderstorms), and under-inflated tires increase blowout risk on hot pavement. Check pressure monthly at minimum — always when tires are cold (morning).

Toll and Traffic Must-Haves

8. SunPass PRO Transponder (Essential for Florida Driving)

The SunPass PRO Transponder covers all Florida toll roads plus toll roads in 21 other states. Tampa Bay has toll roads everywhere — the Selmon Expressway, Veterans Expressway, and Suncoast Parkway all require electronic tolling.

Why it's essential: Many Florida toll plazas are cashless — no booth, no attendant, no cash option. Without a SunPass, you get a toll-by-plate bill at a higher rate, mailed to whatever address is on your registration. Get a SunPass within your first week. The PRO version works if you travel out of state and is compatible with E-ZPass, TxTag, and other systems.

Pro tip: The mini SunPass sticker version adheres to your windshield and is less likely to be stolen than the portable transponder that sits on your dash.

9. Anker 535 Car Charger (Best USB Car Charger)

The Anker 535 Car Charger provides fast USB-C and USB-A charging for your phone and passengers' devices. The 67W output fast-charges even laptops.

Why it matters for Florida: Navigation apps (Waze, Google Maps) drain your battery fast, especially in Tampa Bay traffic. You're also likely running Waze during hurricane evacuations when keeping your phone charged is critical. A quality car charger that actually fast-charges is essential, not optional.

Weather and Storm Protection

10. Covercraft Custom Car Cover (Best for Outdoor Parking)

If you don't have a garage, a Covercraft Custom-Fit Car Cover protects your paint, interior, and rubber seals from Florida's relentless UV exposure and daily afternoon rain. Custom-fit versions don't flap in the wind like universal covers.

Why it works for Florida: Florida sun destroys paint — oxidation, clear coat failure, and fading are accelerated dramatically. If you park outside, a quality cover extends your paint life by years. During hurricane season, a car cover also protects against flying debris during lower-category storms.

11. Rain-X Original Glass Water Repellent (Best for Florida Rain)

Rain-X Original Glass Treatment causes rain to bead and roll off your windshield, dramatically improving visibility during Florida's sudden, heavy downpours.

Why it matters for Florida: Florida afternoon thunderstorms dump rain so hard that wipers on max speed can't keep up. Rain-X treatment means water beads and flies off your windshield at highway speeds. Reapply monthly during rainy season. It's a $7 product that could save your life in a Florida monsoon.

Don't Waste Money On

  • Leather steering wheel wraps — They absorb heat just like the wheel itself. Get a breathable mesh cover instead.
  • Cheap universal sun shades — They don't fit properly, fall down while you're parked, and leave heat gaps. Get one sized for your vehicle.
  • Basic power strip inverters — Most cheap car power inverters produce "modified sine wave" power that can damage sensitive electronics. If you need AC power in your car, invest in a pure sine wave inverter.
  • Magnetic phone mounts in Florida heat — The adhesive on magnetic mounts fails in Florida car temperatures. The magnet detaches from your dash and your phone goes flying. Use a vent-clip mount or a suction mount rated for high temperatures.
  • Fabric seat covers — They absorb sweat and sunscreen, develop mildew in Florida humidity, and smell terrible within months. Neoprene or waterproof materials only.

FAQ

Is window tint legal in Florida?

Yes, with limits. Front side windows must allow at least 28% visible light transmission. Rear side windows and the back window must allow at least 15%. The windshield allows non-reflective tint above the AS-1 line (top 5-6 inches). Medical exemptions are available for darker tints. Get it done professionally — Florida law enforcement does check, and illegal tint is a fixable traffic violation.

Do I need to change my oil more often in Florida?

Florida heat does stress engine oil more than temperate climates. Follow your manufacturer's recommended interval for "severe conditions" (which includes regular high-temperature driving). For most vehicles, that means every 5,000-7,500 miles with synthetic oil, rather than the 10,000-mile intervals some manufacturers suggest for ideal conditions.

Should I get flood insurance for my car in Florida?

Standard auto insurance with comprehensive coverage typically covers flood damage to your vehicle. Review your policy specifically for flood provisions, especially if you live in a flood zone. During hurricane season, insurance companies may stop writing new policies or adding comprehensive coverage when a named storm is approaching — get your coverage settled before June 1.

How do I prepare my car for a Florida hurricane?

Fill your gas tank before the storm (gas stations lose power and run out). Park in a garage if possible. If no garage, park away from trees, power lines, and structures that could fall. Take photos of your car for insurance documentation. Keep your emergency kit stocked. Know your evacuation route — for Tampa Bay, I-75 North or I-4 East are the primary routes. Don't wait until the last minute to leave if ordered to evacuate.

Why do Florida cars rust underneath even though it doesn't snow?

Salt air from the Gulf and Tampa Bay causes undercarriage corrosion, especially for cars parked near the coast. Coastal salt spray is different from road salt but still corrosive. If you live within 10 miles of the water (most of Tampa Bay), wash your undercarriage periodically and consider an undercoating treatment, especially for trucks and SUVs you plan to keep long-term.
